n conclusion,  give a paragraph from the very valuable   ntroduction to the riticism of the
ew estament , by r. . . crivener,   one of the ablest of living biblical scholars and critics—
“rigen is the most celebrated biblical critic of antiquity.   is is the highest name among the
critics and epositors of the early hurch.   e is perpetually engaged in the discussion of various
readings of the ew estament, and employs language, in describing the then eisting state of the
tet, which would be deemed strong if applied even to its present condition, after the changes which
siteen more centuries must needs have produced.…eldom have such warmth of fancy and so
bold a grasp of mind been united with the lifelong, patient industry which procured for this famous
man the honourable appellation of   damantius .”   .]
 
 rologue of ufinus.
————————————
  that very many of the brethren, induced by their thirst for a knowledge of the criptures,
have requested some distinguished men, well versed in reek learning, to translate rigen into
atin, and so make him accessible to oman readers.   mong these, when our brother and
colleague   had, at the earnest entreaty of ishop amasus, translated two of the omilies on the
ong of ongs out of reek into atin, he prefied so elegant and noble a preface to that work, as
to inspire every one with a most eager desire to read and study rigen, saying that the epression,
“he ing hath brought me into his chamber,”   was appropriate to his feelings, and declaring
that while rigen in his other works surpassed all writers, he in the ong of ongs surpassed even
himself.   e promises, indeed, in that very preface, that he will present the books on the ong of
ongs, and numerous others of the works of rigen, in a atin translation, to oman readers.   ut
he, finding greater pleasure in compositions of his own, pursues an end that is attended with greater
fame, viz., in being the author rather than the translator of works.   ccordingly we enter upon the
undertaking, which was thus begun and approved of by him, although we cannot compose in a style
of elegance equal to that of a man of such distinguished eloquence and therefore  am afraid lest,
through my fault, the result should follow, that that man, whom he deservedly esteems as the second
teacher of knowledge and wisdom in the hurch after the apostles, should, through the poverty of
my language, appear far inferior to what he is.   nd this consideration, which frequently recurred
to my mind, kept me silent, and prevented me from yielding to the numerous entreaties of my
   hird edition, ambridge, , pp. , .
   erome is the person alluded to.
   ant. i. .

brethren, until your influence, my very faithful brother acarius, which is so great, rendered it
impossible for my unskilfulness any longer to offer resistance.   nd therefore, that  might not find
you too grievous an eactor,  gave way, even contrary to my resolution on the condition and
arrangement, however, that in my translation  should follow as far as possible the rule observed
by my predecessors, and especially by that distinguished man whom  have mentioned above, who,
after translating into atin more than seventy of those treatises of rigen which are styled   omilies
 and a considerable number also of his writings on the apostles, in which a good many
“stumbling-blocks” are found in the original reek, so smoothed and corrected them in his
translation, that a atin reader would meet with nothing which could appear discordant with our
belief.   is eample, therefore, we follow, to the best of our ability if not with equal power of
eloquence, yet at least with the same strictness of rule, taking care not to reproduce those epressions
occurring in the works of rigen which are inconsistent with and opposed to each other.   he cause
of these variations we have eplained more freely in the   pologeticus , which amphilus wrote in
defence of the works of rigen, where we added a brief tract, in which we showed,  think, by
unmistakeable proofs, that his books had been corrupted in numerous places by heretics and
malevolent persons, and especially those books of which you now require me to undertake the
translation, i.e., the books which may be entitled   e rincipiis   or   e rincipatibus , and which are
indeed in other respects full of obscurities and difficulties.   or he there discusses those subjects
with respect to which philosophers, after spending all their lives upon them, have been unable to
discover anything.   ut here our author strove, as much as in him lay, to turn to the service of
religion the belief in a reator, and the rational nature of created beings, which the latter had
degraded to purposes of wickedness.   f, therefore, we have found anywhere in his writings, any
statement opposed to that view, which elsewhere in his works he had himself piously laid down
regarding the rinity, we have either omitted it, as being corrupt, and not the composition of rigen,
or we have brought it forward agreeably to the rule which we frequently find affirmed by himself.
 
 f, indeed, in his desire to pass rapidly on, he has, as speaking to persons of skill and knowledge,
sometimes epressed himself obscurely, we have, in order that the passage might be clearer, added
what we had read more fully stated on the same subject in his other works, keeping eplanation in
view, but adding nothing of our own, but simply restoring to him what was his, although occurring
in other portions of his writings.
hese remarks, therefore, by way of admonition,  have made in the preface, lest slanderous
individuals perhaps should think that they had a second time discovered matter of accusation.   ut
let perverse and disputatious men have a care what they are about.   or we have in the meantime
undertaken this heavy labour, if od should aid your prayers, not to shut the mouths of slanderers
(which is impossible, although od perhaps will do it), but to afford material to those who desire
to advance in the knowledge of these things.   nd, verily, in the presence of od the ather, and
of the on, and of the oly pirit,  adjure and beseech every one, who may either transcribe or
read these books, by his belief in the kingdom to come, by the mystery of the resurrection from the
dead, and by that everlasting fire prepared for the devil and his angels, that, as he would not possess

for an eternal inheritance that place where there is weeping and gnashing of teeth, and where their
fire is not quenched and their worm dieth not, he add nothing to cripture, and take nothing away
from it, and make no insertion or alteration, but that he compare his transcript with the copies from
which he made it, and make the emendations and distinctions according to the letter, and not have
his manuscript incorrect or indistinct, lest the difficulty of ascertaining the sense, from the
indistinctness of the copy, should cause greater difficulties to the readers.
 
 rigen e rincipiis.
————————————
reface.
.    who believe and are assured that grace and truth were obtained through esus hrist,
and who know hrist to be the truth, agreeably to is own declaration, “ am the truth,”   derive
the knowledge which incites men to a good and happy life from no other source than from the very
words and teaching of hrist.   nd by the words of hrist we do not mean those only which e
spake when e became man and tabernacled in the flesh for before that time, hrist, the ord of
od, was in oses and the prophets.   or without the ord of od, how could they have been
able to prophesy of hrist   nd were it not our purpose to confine the present treatise within the
limits of all attainable brevity, it would not be difficult to show, in proof of this statement, out of
the oly criptures, how oses or the prophets both spake and performed all they did through
being filled with the pirit of hrist.   nd therefore  think it sufficient to quote this one testimony
of aul from the pistle to the ebrews,   in which he says   “y faith oses, when he was come
to years, refused to be called the son of haraoh’s daughter choosing rather to suffer affliction
with the people of od, than to enjoy the pleasures of sin for a season esteeming the reproach of
hrist greater riches than the treasures of the gyptians.”    oreover, that after is ascension
   ohn iv. .
   ere, and frequently elsewhere (some two hundred times in all), rigen, in his etant works, ascribes the authorship of
the pistle to the ebrews to t. aul.   usebius ( cclesiastical istory , vi. ) quotes rigen as saying, “y opinion is this
 the thoughts are the apostle’s but the diction and phraseology belong to some one who has recorded what the apostle said, and
as one who noted down what his master dictated.   f, then, any hurch considers this pistle as coming from aul, let it be
commended for this for neither did those ancient men deliver it as such without cause.   ut who it was that committed the pistle
to writing, is known only to od.”   .]
   eb. i. –.

into heaven e spake in is apostles, is shown by aul in these words   “r do you seek a proof
of hrist who speaketh in me” 
.   ince many, however, of those who profess to believe in hrist differ from each other, not
only in small and trifling matters, but also on subjects of the highest importance, as, e.g., regarding
od, or the ord esus hrist, or the oly pirit and not only regarding these, but also regarding
others which are created eistences, viz., the powers   and the holy virtues   it seems on that
account necessary first of all to fi a definite limit and to lay down an unmistakable rule regarding
each one of these, and then to pass to the investigation of other points.   or as we ceased to seek
for truth (notwithstanding the professions of many among reeks and arbarians to make it known)
among all who claimed it for erroneous opinions, after we had come to believe that hrist was the
on of od, and were persuaded that we must learn it from imself so, seeing there are many who
think they hold the opinions of hrist, and yet some of these think differently from their predecessors,
yet as the teaching of the hurch, transmitted in orderly succession from the apostles, and remaining
in the hurches to the present day, is still preserved, that alone is to be accepted as truth which
differs in no respect from ecclesiastical and apostolical tradition.
.   ow it ought to be known that the holy apostles, in preaching the faith of hrist, delivered
themselves with the utmost clearness on certain points which they believed to be necessary to every
one, even to those who seemed somewhat dull in the investigation of divine knowledge leaving,
however, the grounds of their statements to be eamined into by those who should deserve the
ecellent gifts of the pirit, and who, especially by means of the oly pirit imself, should obtain
the gift of language, of wisdom, and of knowledge   while on other subjects they merely stated the
fact that things were so, keeping silence as to the manner or origin of their eistence clearly in
order that the more zealous of their successors, who should be lovers of wisdom, might have a
subject of eercise on which to display the fruit of their talents,—those persons,  mean, who should
prepare themselves to be fit and worthy receivers of wisdom.
 
 .   he particular points   clearly delivered in the teaching of the apostles are as follow—
 irst , hat there is one od, who created and arranged all things, and who, when nothing
eisted, called all things into being—od from the first creation and foundation of the world—the
od of all just men, of dam, bel, eth, nos, noch, oe, ere, braham, saac, acob, the
twelve patriarchs, oses, and the prophets and that this od in the last days, as e had announced
beforehand by is prophets, sent our ord esus hrist to call in the first place srael to imself,
and in the second place the entiles, after the unfaithfulness of the people of srael.   his just and

good od, the ather of our ord esus hrist, imself gave the law and the prophets, and the
ospels, being also the od of the apostles and of the ld and ew estaments.
 econdly , hat esus hrist imself, who came (into the world), was born of the ather before
all creatures that, after e had been the servant of the ather in the creation of all things—“or
by im were all things made”  —e in the last times, divesting imself (of is glory), became
a man, and was incarnate although od, and while made a man remained the od which e was
that e assumed a body like to our own, differing in this respect only, that it was born of a virgin
and of the oly pirit   that this esus hrist was truly born, and did truly suffer, and did not endure
this death common (to man) in appearance only, but did truly die that e did truly rise from the
dead and that after is resurrection e conversed with is disciples, and was taken up (into
heaven).
hen,   hirdly , the apostles related that the oly pirit was associated in honour and dignity
with the ather and the on.   ut in is case it is not clearly distinguished whether e is to be
regarded as born or innate,   or also as a on of od or not   for these are points which have to be
inquired into out of sacred cripture according to the best of our ability, and which demand careful
investigation.   nd that this pirit inspired each one of the saints, whether prophets or apostles
and that there was not one pirit in the men of the old dispensation, and another in those who were
inspired at the advent of hrist, is most clearly taught throughout the hurches.
.   fter these points, also, the apostolic teaching is that the soul, having a substance   and
life of its own, shall, after its departure from the world, be rewarded according to its deserts, being
destined to obtain either an inheritance of eternal life and blessedness, if its actions shall have
procured this for it, or to be delivered up to eternal fire and punishments, if the guilt of its crimes
shall have brought it down to this   and also, that there is to be a time of resurrection from the dead,
when this body, which now “is sown in corruption, shall rise in incorruption,” and that which “is
sown in dishonour will rise in glory.”    his also is clearly defined in the teaching of the hurch,
that every rational soul is possessed of free-will and volition that it has a struggle to maintain with
the devil and his angels, and opposing influences,   because they strive to burden it with sins but
if we live rightly and wisely, we should endeavour to shake ourselves free of a burden of that kind.
 rom which it follows, also, that we understand ourselves not to be subject to necessity, so as to

 alias   ), “factus an infectus.”   riticising the errors in the first book of the   rinciples , he says   “rigen declares the oly pirit
to be third in dignity and honour after the ather and the on and although professing ignorance whether he were created or not
(factus an infectus), he indicated afterwards his opinion regarding him, maintaining that nothing was uncreated ecept od the
ather.”   erome, no doubt, read   γενητὸς   ἢ   ἀγένητος , and ufinus   γεννητὸς   ἢ   ἀγέννητος .—.

be compelled by all means, even against our will, to do either good or evil.   or if we are our own
masters, some influences perhaps may impel us to sin, and others help us to salvation we are not
forced, however, by any necessity either to act rightly or wrongly, which those persons think is the
case who say that the courses and movements of the stars are the cause of human actions, not only
of those which take place beyond the influence of the freedom of the will, but also of those which
are placed within our own power.   ut with respect to the soul, whether it is derived from the seed
by a process of traducianism, so that the reason or substance of it may be considered as placed in
the seminal particles of the body themselves, or whether it has any other beginning and this
beginning, itself, whether it be by birth or not, or whether bestowed upon the body from without
or no, is not distinguished with sufficient clearness in the teaching of the hurch.
.   egarding the devil and his angels, and the opposing influences, the teaching of the hurch
has laid down that these beings eist indeed but what they are, or how they eist, it has not eplained
with sufficient clearness.   his opinion, however, is held by most, that the devil was an angel, and
that, having become an apostate, he induced as many of the angels as possible to fall away with
himself, and these up to the present time are called his angels.
.   his also is a part of the hurch’s teaching, that the world was made and took its beginning
 
 at a certain time, and is to be destroyed on account of its wickedness.   ut what eisted before this
world, or what will eist after it, has not become certainly known to the many, for there is no clear
statement regarding it in the teaching of the hurch.
.   hen, finally, that the criptures were written by the pirit of od, and have a meaning, not
such only as is apparent at first sight, but also another, which escapes the notice of most.   or those
(words) which are written are the forms of certain mysteries,   and the images of divine things.
 especting which there is one opinion throughout the whole hurch, that the whole law is indeed
spiritual but that the spiritual meaning which the law conveys is not known to all, but to those only
on whom the grace of the oly pirit is bestowed in the word of wisdom and knowledge.
he term   ἀσώματον , i.e., incorporeal, is disused and unknown, not only in many other writings,
but also in our own criptures.   nd if any one should quote it to us out of the little treatise entitled
 he octrine of eter ,   in which the aviour seems to say to is disciples, “ am not an incorporeal
demon,”    have to reply, in the first place, that that work is not included among ecclesiastical
   acramentorum.
   usebius ( ist. ccles ., iii. c. ), treating of gnatius, quotes from his pistle to the hurch of myrna as follows
 “riting to the myrnæans, he (gnatius) has employed words respecting esus,  know not whence they are taken, to the
following effect   ‘ut  know and believe that e was seen after the resurrection and when e came to eter and his companions,
e said to them, ake and handle e, and see that  am not an incorporeal spirit.’”   erome, in his catalogue of ecclesiastical
writers, says the words are a quotation from the ospel of the azarenes, a work which he had recently translated.   rigen here
quotes them, however, from   he octrine of eter , on which uæus remarks that the words might be contained in both of these
apocryphal works.
   æmonium.

books for we can show that it was not composed either by eter or by any other person inspired
by the pirit of od.   ut even if the point were to be conceded, the word   ἀσώματον   there does
not convey the same meaning as is intended by reek and entile authors when incorporeal nature
is discussed by philosophers.   or in the little treatise referred to he used the phrase “incorporeal
demon” to denote that that form or outline of demoniacal body, whatever it is, does not resemble
this gross and visible body of ours but, agreeably to the intention of the author of the treatise, it
must be understood to mean that e had not such a body as demons have, which is naturally fine, 
and thin as if formed of air (and for this reason is either considered or called by many incorporeal),
but that e had a solid and palpable body.   ow, according to human custom, everything which is
not of that nature is called by the simple or ignorant incorporeal as if one were to say that the air
which we breathe was incorporeal, because it is not a body of such a nature as can be grasped and
held, or can offer resistance to pressure.
.   e shall inquire, however, whether the thing which reek philosophers call   ἀσώματον , or
“incorporeal,” is found in holy cripture under another name.   or it is also to be a subject of
investigation how od himself is to be understood,—whether as corporeal, and formed according
to some shape, or of a different nature from bodies,—a point which is not clearly indicated in our
teaching.   nd the same inquiries have to be made regarding hrist and the oly pirit, as well as
respecting every soul, and everything possessed of a rational nature.
.   his also is a part of the teaching of the hurch, that there are certain angels of od, and
certain good influences, which are is servants in accomplishing the salvation of men.   hen these,
however, were created, or of what nature they are, or how they eist, is not clearly stated.   egarding
the sun, moon, and stars, whether they are living beings or without life, there is no distinct
deliverance. 
very one, therefore, must make use of elements and foundations of this sort, according to the
precept, “nlighten yourselves with the light of knowledge,”   if he would desire to form a
connected series and body of truths agreeably to the reason of all these things, that by clear and
necessary statements he may ascertain the truth regarding each individual topic, and form, as we
have said, one body of doctrine, by means of illustrations and arguments,—either those which he
has discovered in holy cripture, or which he has deduced by closely tracing out the consequences
and following a correct method.

hapter .—n od.
.     that some will attempt to say that, even according to the declarations of our own
criptures, od is a body, because in the writings of oses they find it said, that “our od is a
consuming fire”   and in the ospel according to ohn, that “od is a pirit, and they who worship
im must worship im in spirit and in truth.”    ire and spirit, according to them, are to be
regarded as nothing else than a body.   ow,  should like to ask these persons what they have to
say respecting that passage where it is declared that od is light as ohn writes in his pistle, “od
is light, and in im there is no darkness at all.”    ruly e is that light which illuminates the
whole understanding of those who are capable of receiving truth, as is said in the thirty-sith salm,
“n hy light we shall see light.”    or what other light of od can be named, “in which any one
sees light,” save an influence of od, by which a man, being enlightened, either thoroughly sees
the truth of all things, or comes to know od imself, who is called the truth   uch is the meaning
of the epression, “n hy light we shall see light” i.e., in hy word and wisdom which is hy
on, in imself we shall see hee the ather.   ecause e is called light, shall e be supposed to
have any resemblance to the light of the sun   r how should there be the slightest ground for
imagining, that from that corporeal light any one could derive the cause of knowledge, and come
to the understanding of the truth
.   f, then, they acquiesce in our assertion, which reason itself has demonstrated, regarding the
nature of light, and acknowledge that od cannot be understood to be a body in the sense that light
is, similar reasoning will hold true of the epression “a consuming fire.”   or what will od consume
in respect of is being fire   hall e be thought to consume material substance, as wood, or hay,
or stubble   nd what in this view can be called worthy of the glory of od, if e be a fire,
consuming materials of that kind   ut let us reflect that od does indeed consume and utterly
destroy that e consumes evil thoughts, wicked actions, and sinful desires, when they find their
way into the minds of believers and that, inhabiting along with is on those souls which are
rendered capable of receiving is word and wisdom, according to is own declaration, “ and the
ather shall come, and e shall make our abode with him”    e makes them, after all their vices
and passions have been consumed, a holy temple, worthy of imself.   hose, moreover, who, on

account of the epression “od is a pirit,” think that e is a body, are to be answered,  think, in
the following manner.   t is the custom of sacred cripture, when it wishes to designate anything
opposed to this gross and solid body, to call it spirit, as in the epression, “he letter killeth, but
the spirit giveth life,”   where there can be no doubt that by “letter” are meant bodily things, and
by “spirit” intellectual things, which we also term “spiritual.”   he apostle, moreover, says, “ven
unto this day, when oses is read, the veil is upon their heart   nevertheless, when it shall turn to
the ord, the veil shall be taken away   and where the pirit of the ord is, there is liberty.”    or
so long as any one is not converted to a spiritual understanding, a veil is placed over his heart, with
which veil, i.e., a gross understanding, cripture itself is said or thought to be covered   and this is
the meaning of the statement that a veil was placed over the countenance of oses when he spoke
to the people, i.e., when the law was publicly read aloud.   ut if we turn to the ord, where also is
the word of od, and where the oly pirit reveals spiritual knowledge, then the veil is taken away,
and with unveiled face we shall behold the glory of the ord in the holy criptures.
.   nd since many saints participate in the oly pirit, e cannot therefore be understood to
be a body, which being divided into corporeal parts, is partaken of by each one of the saints but
e is manifestly a sanctifying power, in which all are said to have a share who have deserved to
be sanctified by is grace.   nd in order that what we say may be more easily understood, let us
take an illustration from things very dissimilar.   here are many persons who take a part in the
science   or art of medicine   are we therefore to suppose that those who do so take to themselves
the particles of some body called medicine, which is placed before them, and in this way participate
in the same   r must we not rather understand that all who with quick and trained minds come to
 
 understand the art and discipline itself, may be said to be partakers of the art of healing   ut these
are not to be deemed altogether parallel instances in a comparison of medicine to the oly pirit,
as they have been adduced only to establish that that is not necessarily to be considered a body, a
share in which is possessed by many individuals.   or the oly pirit differs widely from the method
or science of medicine, in respect that the oly pirit is an intellectual eistence   and subsists
and eists in a peculiar manner, whereas medicine is not at all of that nature.
.   ut we must pass on to the language of the ospel itself, in which it is declared that “od
is a pirit,” and where we have to show how that is to be understood agreeably to what we have
stated.   or let us inquire on what occasion these words were spoken by the aviour, before whom
e uttered them, and what was the subject of investigation.   e find, without any doubt, that e
spoke these words to the amaritan woman, saying to her, who thought, agreeably to the amaritan
view, that od ought to be worshipped on ount erizim, that “od is a pirit.”   or the amaritan
woman, believing im to be a ew, was inquiring of im whether od ought to be worshipped in

   ubsistentia.

erusalem or on this mountain and her words were, “ll our fathers worshipped on this mountain,
and ye say that in erusalem is the place where we ought to worship.”    o this opinion of the
amaritan woman, therefore, who imagined that od was less rightly or duly worshipped, according
to the privileges of the different localities, either by the ews in erusalem or by the amaritans on
ount erizim, the aviour answered that he who would follow the ord must lay aside all
preference for particular places, and thus epressed imself   “he hour is coming when neither
in erusalem nor on this mountain shall the true worshippers worship the ather.   od is a pirit,
and they who worship im must worship im in spirit and in truth.”    nd observe how logically
e has joined together the spirit and the truth   e called od a pirit, that e might distinguish
im from bodies and e named im the truth, to distinguish im from a shadow or an image.
 or they who worshipped in erusalem worshipped od neither in truth nor in spirit, being in
subjection to the shadow or image of heavenly things and such also was the case with those who
worshipped on ount erizim.
.   aving refuted, then, as well as we could, every notion which might suggest that we were
to think of od as in any degree corporeal, we go on to say that, according to strict truth, od is
incomprehensible, and incapable of being measured.    or whatever be the knowledge which we
are able to obtain of od, either by perception or reflection, we must of necessity believe that e
is by many degrees far better than what we perceive im to be.   or, as if we were to see any one
unable to bear a spark of light, or the flame of a very small lamp, and were desirous to acquaint

also when we proved that from the food either of men or animals the substance of the flesh was
derived, or that the moisture of the natural seed was converted into solid flesh and bones—all
which go to prove that the substance of the body is changeable, and may pass from one quality into
all others.
.   evertheless we must not forget that a substance never eists without a quality, and that
it is by an act of the understanding alone that this (substance) which underlies bodies, and which
is capable of quality, is discovered to be matter.   ome indeed, in their desire to investigate these
subjects more profoundly, have ventured to assert that bodily nature   is nothing else than qualities.

will appear to be “qualities.”   nd therefore also those persons who make these assertions have
endeavoured to maintain, that since all who say that matter was uncreated will admit that qualities
were created by od, it may be in this way shown that even according to them matter was not
uncreated since qualities constitute everything, and these are declared by all without contradiction
to have been made by od.   hose, again, who would make out that qualities are superimposed

doubtedly is either silent, or speaks, or watches, or sleeps, or maintains a certain attitude of body

to men, without which they are almost never found.   nd yet our conception of man does not lay

means, that we do not at all take into account the reason of his (particular) condition either in
watching, or in sleeping, or in speaking, or in keeping silence, or in any other action that must
necessarily happen to men.    f any one, then, can regard aul as being without all these things
which are capable of happening, he will in the same way also be able to understand this underlying
(substance) without qualities.   hen, then, our mind puts away all qualities from its conception,

substance, then by means of this somewhat simulated process of thought   it will appear to behold
matter clear from qualities of every kind.

require it as a covering   for there will be always rational natures which need a bodily covering
and there will therefore always be a bodily nature whose coverings must necessarily be used by
rational creatures, unless some one be able to demonstrate by arguments that a rational nature can
live without a body.   ut how difficult—nay, how almost impossible—this is for our understanding,
we have shown in the preceding pages, in our discussion of the individual topics.
.   t will not,  consider, be opposed to the nature of our undertaking, if we restate with all
 
 possible brevity our opinions on the immortality of rational natures.   very one who participates
in anything, is unquestionably of one essence and nature with him who is partaker of the same
thing.   or eample, as all eyes participate in the light, so accordingly all eyes which partake of the
light are of one nature but although every eye partakes of the light, yet, inasmuch as one sees more
clearly, and another more obscurely, every eye does not equally share in the light.   nd again, all
hearing receives voice or sound, and therefore all hearing is of one nature but each one hears more
rapidly or more slowly, according as the quality of his hearing is clear and sound.   et us pass now
from these sensuous illustrations to the consideration of intellectual things.   very mind which
partakes of intellectual light ought undoubtedly to be of one nature with every mind which partakes
in a similar manner of intellectual light.   f the heavenly virtues, then, partake of intellectual light,
i.e., of divine nature, because they participate in wisdom and holiness, and if human souls, have
partaken of the same light and wisdom, and thus are mutually of one nature and of one
essence,—then, since the heavenly virtues are incorruptible and immortal, the essence of the human
soul will also be immortal and incorruptible.   nd not only so, but because the nature of ather,
and on, and oly pirit, whose intellectual light alone all created things have a share, is
incorruptible and eternal, it is altogether consistent and necessary that every substance which
partakes of that eternal nature should last for ever, and be incorruptible and eternal, so that the
eternity of divine goodness may be understood also in this respect, that they who obtain its benefits
are also eternal.   ut as, in the instances referred to, a diversity in the participation of the light was
observed, when the glance of the beholder was described as being duller or more acute, so also a
diversity is to be noted in the participation of ather, on, and oly pirit, varying with the degree
of zeal or capacity of mind.   f such were not the case,   we have to consider whether it would not
seem to be an act of impiety to say that the mind which is capable of (receiving) od should admit
of a destruction of its essence   as if the very fact that it is able to feel and understand od could
not suffice for its perpetual eistence, especially since, if even through neglect the mind fall away
from a pure and complete reception of od, it nevertheless contains within it certain seeds of
restoration and renewal to a better understanding, seeing the “inner,” which is also called the
“rational” man, is renewed after “the image and likeness of od, who created him.”   nd therefore
